mnemonics.title: Random Quicksort Steps
mnemonics.content: PARI: Partition → Apply randomly → Recurse → Integrate results. Remember: Pick Pivot, Arrange (partition), Repeat on sub-arrays.

mnemonics.title: Quicksort Complexity
mnemonics.content: AVG: Always Very Good - Average O(n log n); WORST: Only Poorly - O(n²). RAND: Really Good - Expected O(n log n) with low O(n²) probability.

mnemonics.title: Partition Remember
mnemonics.content: Lomuto partition: i tracks last smaller, j scans all. When arr[j] < pivot, increment i and swap arr[i],arr[j]. Finally swap pivot with arr[i+1].

acronyms.usage: Sorting algorithm using random pivot selection for O(n log n) expected time
Original partition scheme for Quicksort using two pointers approach
Hybrid algorithm combining Quicksort, Heapsort, and Insertion Sort
Common partition implementation with one pivot at end

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hotel hallway with guests (elements). The pivot is the 'host' standing in the middle. Guests shorter than host go to left, taller to right. Finally, host finds their correct position.
Think of picking a random person from a queue to be the 'judge' who divides people by height. Different judges give different but generally balanced divisions.
Draw an upside-down tree. Root is first pivot, branches are subarrays. Random pivots create balanced branches like a healthy tree; bad pivots create lopsided branches.

analogies.analogy: Imagine sorting books by height. Pick a random book as reference, arrange others to left (shorter) or right (taller). Repeat for each section. Random selection prevents slow sorting if books are already sorted.
Coach randomly picks a player as team captain. Captain divides remaining players into two teams: shorter and taller. This process repeats, ensuring fair teams on average.
In a race, random judges (pivots) stand at different checkpoints. Each judge (pivot) sends runners to left or right based on their speed. Most judges create balanced groups.
